channel.title: Terms on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Terms on emsenn.net

item.title: Immanence
item.description:
Immanence is the claim that causes, structures, and standards of critique are internal to the forms being analyzed.

In dialectical method, immanent critique evaluates a position using that position’s own claims and commitments, showing how internal tensions produce revision or breakdown.

In metaphysics, immanence is often contrasted with transcendence: explanation is sought within being, relation, or process itself, not in an external principle standing outside the world.

¶Related terms

  • Ontology
  • Metaphysics
  • Dialectics
  • Mediation

item.title: Contingency
item.description:
Contingency is the condition of existing without necessity — something is contingent when it exists but could have been otherwise. A contingent thing depends on conditions that could have been different. It is not random — a contingent arrangement has specific, traceable causes — but those causes were not inevitable, and different causes would have produced a different arrangement.

The concept operates at multiple levels. Metaphysically, contingency names the difference between things that must exist (if anything must — mathematical truths, logical laws, depending on one’s commitments) and things that happen to exist because of the specific way the world unfolded. The English language is contingent: had history gone differently, the people of the British Isles might speak a different language or no common language at all. The laws of thermodynamics are (arguably) necessary: they hold regardless of historical accident.

item.title: Materialism
item.description:
Materialism, in philosophy, is the position that the physical world is what fundamentally exists. Ideas, culture, consciousness, institutions — all of these are real, but they arise from material conditions rather than the other way around. When a materialist asks why a society has the values it does, they look at how that society produces food, shelter, and goods, not at what its philosophers wrote.

This contrasts with idealism, which holds that mind, spirit, or ideas are more fundamental than matter. Georg Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel, the most influential idealist, argued that history is the unfolding of Spirit coming to know itself through stages of consciousness. For Hegel, ideas shape the material world. Materialism inverts this: the material world shapes ideas.

item.title: Metaphysics
item.description: Metaphysics asks: what is the world made of, at the deepest level? Not what particular things exist (that’s a job for science) but what kind of thing reality is. Is the world fundamentally made of matter? Of mind? Of processes? Of relations? When a physicist describes subatomic particles, metaphysics asks what it means for those particles to be — what assumptions about reality the physicist is already making before the first measurement.
